questions.text: What is the main purpose of an operating system?

options.text: To run games and productivity applications
To manage hardware resources and provide an interface for users and applications
To store files permanently on the hard drive
To connect the computer to the internet

questions.explanation: An OS manages hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage, peripherals) and provides an interface so users and applications can interact with the hardware without needing to know hardware specifics.

options.text: Running one program with multiple threads
Using multiple monitors simultaneously
The OS rapidly switching the CPU between processes so they appear to run simultaneously
Having multiple users logged in at once

questions.explanation: Multi-tasking is the OS rapidly switching the CPU's attention between processes. The switching is so fast it appears simultaneous to users.

options.text: An error that crashes the operating system
A signal to the CPU requesting immediate attention, pausing the current process
A way for the user to pause a running program
A timer that limits how long each program can run

questions.explanation: An interrupt is a signal from hardware or software that causes the CPU to pause its current task, handle the interrupt, then resume. This allows events like keypresses to be processed immediately.

options.text: A hardware component inside a printer
Software that allows the OS to communicate with a specific peripheral
A type of storage device
A program that manages USB connections

questions.explanation: A device driver translates the OS's generic commands into device-specific instructions. Without drivers, the OS would need custom code for every piece of hardware.

options.text: Memory stored on a remote server
Extra-fast memory inside the CPU
A section of secondary storage used as an extension of RAM when RAM is full
RAM that is allocated but not currently being used

questions.explanation: When physical RAM is full, the OS uses part of the hard drive/SSD as virtual memory. This is slower than RAM but prevents the system from running out of memory.

questions.text: What is an advantage of a Command Line Interface (CLI) over a Graphical User Interface (GUI)?

options.text: Easier for beginners
No need to remember commands
Uses more system resources
Faster for experienced users and allows scripting to automate tasks

questions.explanation: CLI is faster for experienced users and commands can be chained into scripts for automation. GUIs are more intuitive for beginners but use more system resources.

questions.text: Which utility software would you use to improve the performance of a fragmented HDD?

options.text: Antivirus software
Disk defragmenter
Firewall
Backup software

questions.explanation: A disk defragmenter rearranges fragmented files on a HDD so related data is stored contiguously, reducing the time the read head needs to move. Not needed for SSDs.

questions.text: What is the difference between system software and application software?

options.text: System software is written in Python; application software uses C++
System software manages the computer; application software performs tasks for the user
System software runs on mobile devices; application software runs on desktops
There is no difference; they are interchangeable terms

questions.explanation: System software (OS, drivers, utilities) manages the hardware and provides a platform. Application software (browsers, word processors) uses that platform to perform user tasks.

questions.text: How does the OS protect users' data from other applications on the same system?

options.text: By encrypting all files automatically
By preventing applications from accessing memory space allocated to other processes
By requiring administrator approval for all operations
By running each application on a separate CPU core

questions.explanation: The OS's memory management prevents one process from reading or writing to another process's memory space, protecting data integrity and security.

questions.text: Why is a disk defragmenter NOT needed for an SSD?

options.text: SSDs are too slow to benefit from defragmentation
SSDs store data in the same way as HDDs
SSDs have no moving read/write head, so access time is uniform regardless of data location
SSDs automatically defragment themselves

questions.explanation: HDDs benefit from defragmentation because a moving read head has to physically travel to fragmented data. SSDs have no moving parts — every location is accessed equally fast, so fragmentation doesn't cause performance loss.
